title: Carrier-coupled ultrafast structural dynamics and interlayer energy transport of supported transition metal dichalcogenide heterostructures
authors: Md. Shaikot Alam Shakil, Ting-Hsuan Wu, Xing He, Abu Montakim Tareq, Zhenjia Zhou, Libo Gao, Naihao Chiang, Ding-Shyue Yang
abstract: Understanding the electronic coupling and energy flow across layered two-dimensional heterostructures (HSs) is crucial to the exploitation of carrier and phonon transports as well as thermal management in next-generation optoelectronic devices. By using reflection ultrafast electron diffraction, we directly examine photoinduced out-of-plane structural dynamics of supported MoS2/WS2 bilayer HSs and their individual monolayers. Experimental evidence reveals the launch of ultrafast carrier-coupled intralayer atomic motions due to interlayer charge transfer across the van der Waals (vdW) heterojunctions that is absent for individual monolayers. Such a notable carrier-lattice correlation is in addition to the electronic coupling manifested in the enhanced optical absorption for HSs. Also, different pathways of energy flow as a result of carrier-phonon coupling and phonon scattering are reported with the corresponding characteristic times. On longer timescales, relaxation of thermalized atomic motions can be sufficiently described by a thermal transport model. A higher thermal boundary conductance (TBC) across MoS2/WS2 HSs is obtained compared to those at the monolayer-substrate interfaces; however, the similar TBC values suggest comparable couplings of phonons across vdW contacts. These results further shed light on the optical, phonon, and interfacial thermal properties of vertically-stacked vdW HSs.
summary: This work investigates how energy and structure evolve in stacked 2D material heterostructures ($	ext{MoS}_2/	ext{WS}_2$) following intense laser excitation. Using ultrafast electron diffraction and thermal modeling, the authors demonstrate that interlayer coupling creates unique, ultrafast structural dynamics and influences heat dissipation across the interfaces. These findings are vital for engineering next-generation optoelectronic devices.
main_problem: The study aims to understand the electronic coupling and energy flow (carrier and phonon transport) across layered two-dimensional (2D) heterostructures for use in next-generation optoelectronic devices.
main_result: The heterostructure exhibits ultrafast, carrier-coupled structural dynamics absent in individual monolayers, and the interlayer thermal boundary conductance (TBC) is comparable to, and potentially higher than, monolayer-substrate interfaces.
method: The research combines ultrafast electron diffraction (UED) to probe structural dynamics with thermal transport modeling to analyze energy dissipation.
model_or_system: The physical system is supported Transition Metal Dichalcogenide (TMD) heterostructures, specifically $	ext{MoS}_2/	ext{WS}_2$ bilayers, studied via photoexcitation. The analysis involves modeling heat diffusion across multiple interfaces using coupled partial differential equations.
key_observables: Photoinduced out-of-plane structural dynamics, interlayer charge transfer (ICT) dynamics, characteristic time constants ($	au_{	ext{OP}}$, $	au_{	ext{thermal}}$), and thermal boundary conductance (TBC).
important_parameters: Fluence levels ($\sim 3.2 	ext{ mJ cm}^{-2}$), time scales (femtoseconds to picoseconds), and TBC values ($	ext{MW m}^{-2} 	ext{K}^{-1}$).
main_assumptions: The thermal model assumes local intralayer thermal equilibrium, and the UED analysis has limitations regarding probe depth and sensitivity to specific phonon modes.
figures_summary: Figures compare early-time diffraction intensity changes between the heterostructure and individual monolayers, illustrate the energy flow pathways involving ICT, and compare structural recovery dynamics and calculated temperatures using thermal models.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from characterizing the fundamental physical problem (energy flow in 2D HSs) to presenting ultrafast experimental measurements (UED) showing unique carrier-coupled dynamics, and finally applying thermal transport modeling to quantify energy dissipation across interfaces.
why_interesting: The study provides quantitative insights into non-equilibrium energy transfer mechanisms (carrier-phonon coupling) in nanoscale materials, which is crucial for designing efficient energy harvesting and thermal management devices relevant to quantum technologies.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Schematic of reflection UED experiments and diffraction images. (a) A schematic showing the optical excitation of sapphire-supported MoS2/WS2 by a 515-nm pulsed laser and probed by 30-keV electrons at a grazing incidence of ~2.4°. (b) Ordered diffraction streaks with the Miller indices acquired from the top 2D MoS2 layer of the HS using a parallel electron beam. (c) Broadened diffraction streaks as a result of the use of a gently focused electron beam. The image is a reference frame acquired prior to photoexcitation (t < 0). (d) Diffraction differences observed at 20 ps with a laser fluence of 3.2 mJ cm−2. (e) Diffraction intensity profiles of the center streak along the...

Figure 3. Fluence dependence of diffraction changes and photoinduced processes in TMDC materials. (a) Maximum diffraction intensity decreases as a function of photoexcitation fluence for 1L MoS2, 1L WS2, and MoS2/WS2 HSs. Dashed lines are linear fits of the data. (b) Expanded view of the low-fluence range from panel a. (c) Apparent rise time constants ߬୰ ୄ of the individual monolayers and the HS as a function of laser fluence. Dashed lines are guides to the eye. (d) Type-II band alignment and ultrafast ICT in MoS2/WS2 HSs. (e) Schematic of photoinjection of charged carriers (left), carrier–phonon coupling, major phonon modes involved (middle, with black arrows indicating the atomic...

Figure 4. Structural recovery dynamics of TMDC monolayers and HSs at longer times. (a) Time-dependent recovery of diffraction intensities of 1L MoS2, 1L WS2, and the MoS2/WS2 HS measured at 3.2 mJ cm−2. (b) Apparent recovery time constants for the individual monolayers and the HS as a function of excitation fluence. Dashed lines are guides to the eye.

Figure 5. Comparison of experimentally-derived temperature jumps with the thermal transport model of TMDC systems. (a–c) Effective lattice temperatures derived from the UED data (symbols) over excitation fluences ranging from 0.27 to 3.2 mJ cm−2 for (a) 1L MoS2, (b) 1L WS2, and (c) MoS2 HS in the HS. Solid lines are theoretical results. Insets display the corresponding maximum temperature jumps at select fluences, with the standard deviations indicated, as well as the linear dependence (dashed line). (d) Schematic of cross-plane heat dissipation in the supported HS, illustrating the layer thicknesses, the corresponding temperatures, and the two interfacial TBCs.

Figure 6. Comparison of the recovery dynamics of photoexcited MoS2 fabricated as atomic layer deposition (ALD)–grown crystalline (blue; this work), chemical vapor deposition (CVD)–grown crystalline (black; this work), and CVD-grown polycrystalline (red; adapted from28) films. Solid lines represent fits using the thermal transport model.
